items.title: AI is Making You A Shallow Developer
items.description: Key Takeaways
items.content:
Key Takeaways

  • AI is a tool for the informed: It only works if you know what you’re trying to do and how to ask for it.

  • Beware of “Cognitive Debt”: Relying on AI offloads the mental effort, which weakens your neural engagement and makes it harder to remember or explain your own code.

  • The Expertise Barrier: You need a solid foundation to filter out AI’s mistakes; otherwise, it’s just a time-sink.

  • Stay in the driver’s seat: To stay sharp, you must remain a creator, not just an observer. Limit AI to the repetitive “grunt work” and keep the core logic for your own brain.

If you’re not creating, you’re not really learning—and you’re not really living the craft. You’re essentially daydreaming.

The experience of coding with AI is hard to describe, but if I had to use one word, it’s shallow. By contrast, when you are actually writing code, you are fully engaged. You’re constantly learning from failed tests, type-check errors, and integration headaches. When you rely too much on AI, you become detached from that process. Even though you’re still sitting at your desk looking at the monitor, you aren’t really in the work anymore.

The Loss of Joy

I’ve started to feel like AI is taking away the best parts of programming. I’m talking about the interesting work: troubleshooting a production incident like a detective, verifying a quick idea with a console.log, or tweaking CSS box shadows with live reload to get a mockup just right. That fun is disappearing.

Ironically, on average, I’m more “productive” than ever. I can easily write a thousand lines of code, delete them, and write another thousand in a single morning. But I never step into “the zone” anymore. I don’t leave my desk feeling energized or proud of what I built.

AI might not replace you as a developer directly, but it might steal the joy that made you want to be a developer in the first place. And once the work becomes boring, you’ll eventually want to leave the industry altogether.

The Illusion of Expertise

At first glance, LLMs tuned for coding are fun. They are fast, they adapt to your codebase, and they mimic your patterns perfectly. The AI acts like a domain expert, generating code 100 times faster than any human colleague. It turns mockups into components that follow your ESLint rules, writes the tests, and sets up the Storybooks. You just provide feedback and approve the PR.

On the surface, it looks right. But after a few weeks, the patterns you’ve known for years start to feel alien. You look at the code and don’t fully understand why a function exists or why a check is done a certain way.

When you ask the AI for help, the original reasoning is often gone from its “short-term memory” (the session). The logic becomes “magic”—like a contractor who wrote thousands of lines of code and quit without a handoff. Except now, that happens every single minute.

The Accumulation of “Cognitive Debt”

I call this shallow work, and it turns out there is actual science behind this feeling. A recent study from the MIT Media Lab examined how using AI assistants leads to what they call “Cognitive Debt.” Using EEG brain scans, researchers found that people using AI assistants showed significantly weaker neural connectivity in areas tied to deep thinking and memory. Most shockingly, 83% of AI users were unable to quote from the work they had just produced. They had the “output,” but they didn’t have the understanding.

This is exactly what happens in a coding session. You are only observing the surface, which means mistakes easily slip into production. If the AI writes the code and the test, it might pass the test by simply walking around the fundamental issue. If you aren’t hands-on, your brain effectively “offloads” the logic, and you lose your grip on the project.

The Learning Trap

I call this shallow work. You are only observing the surface, which means mistakes easily slip into production. If the AI writes the code and the test, it might pass the test by simply walking around the fundamental issue. If you aren’t hands-on, you won’t catch it.

Real learning isn’t watching someone do something right. It’s the opposite: it’s you trying things that don’t work until you find the way that does. With AI, you just ask it to fix the “shitty things” until you see a good result. You tell yourself, “I’m satisfied; it probably wrote what I would have written.”

But that’s a lie. You learned nothing. You’ve just become an agent that translates business requirements into prompts. It raises the question: Are you still a programmer? Or just a translator?

Read more

items.title: The Messy Path Behind Real Learning
items.description: Why building one small thing end to end teaches more than following a perfect learning path
items.content:
Circling Around a Familiar Problem

I’ve been working on some map-based data visualisation recently.

To be fair, this isn’t completely new to me. Over the past few years, I’ve done similar work before — playing with Blender and DEM data, experimenting with 3D terrain and relief. I did manage to get some results, but none of them felt worth sharing. I roughly understood what was happening, but not deeply enough to feel confident about it.

Looking back, those attempts were more like touching the surface. I knew which buttons to press, but I didn’t really have a clear mental model of what I was doing or why things worked the way they did.

This time, something felt different.

Learning Rarely Follows a Straight Line

What I realised — again, but much more clearly — is that learning is not linear, especially when you enter a domain you’re less familiar with.

It’s unlikely that there’s a clean, well-structured path that tells you exactly what you need to learn and in what order. And even when such a path exists, it’s often boring, abstract, or disconnected from what you actually want to build.

You don’t really learn a new domain by following a syllabus. You learn by getting stuck, trying things, backing out, and exploring different directions. That messiness isn’t a flaw in the process — it is the process.

A Small End-to-End Journey Works Better

What consistently works better, in my experience, is following a simple but complete, end-to-end journey.

Build one thing. Finish it. Then iterate on top of it.

Not perfectly. Not optimally. Just end to end.

This matters because learning isolated concepts without context rarely sticks. When you build something end to end, every detail suddenly has a purpose. You’re no longer learning “because you should”, but because the next step depends on it.

Inspiration, and the Illusion of the Final Result

Recently, I came across some truly beautiful cartography artwork from Viz Art. Even if you’re not interested in maps or GIS, the work itself is worth seeing.

At first, I assumed these pieces were designed physically, printed, and then scanned or photographed into a digital format. And on the other hand that sounds like too much effort, or maybe they have some secret weapon I didn’t know (the unknown unknown).

Anyway, that idea stayed in my head for a while as a small puzzle.

Then one night, while watching a YouTube video about a completely different topic, I noticed something interesting. The creator started from an old map, combined it with DEM data, and stitched everything together to produce a 3D relief.

That was the missing piece for me.

What we usually see online is only the final result — the clean render, the polished artwork. What we don’t see are the hundreds of failed attempts, wrong assumptions, broken files, and abandoned paths behind it.

That invisible exploration is where the real learning happens.

The Messy Reality of Actually Doing the Work

Watching videos on YouTube is usually not the real learning.

To really learn something, you have to do it on purpose: typing, clicking, drawing, mimicking — often clumsily. You will get blocked. You will get frustrated. You will Google things, ask AI, and still find that nothing works.

And then, sometimes, the next morning, you suddenly see a way forward.

For a single visualisation, there are many things involved: colour schemes, 3D rendering, GIS concepts, DEM data, image editing. That means many tools — QGIS, Photoshop (or GIMP), Blender — and each of them has hundreds of details.

In QGIS alone, you run into concepts like CRS, extent, layers, shapefiles, raster operations, clipping, reprojection, GeoTIFFs, pseudo-colour. In image editing, there are hues, masks, channels, blending. In Blender, there are materials, modifiers, subdivision, textures, lights, rendering settings.

There is some overlap, but most skills don’t transfer directly. On top of that, hardware limits become very real once you start working with large DEM files or heavy subdivisions.

Trying to understand everything upfront is overwhelming — and unnecessary.

Name the Process First, Then Fill the Gaps

What helped me most was to stop focusing on mastering tools and instead focus on establishing a process.

I could roughly describe the work in three stages:

  • preparing the data

  • preparing the texture

  • rendering the result

That’s it.

Once you can name the stages, you have a mental model. Without it, it’s easy to get dragged into details too early and lose sight of the goal. At this stage, you only learn what’s necessary to move forward.

Later, once the pipeline exists, curiosity naturally kicks in. You start asking better questions: how DEM data is stored, how colour actually works, how subdivision affects geometry, how light changes the final result.

This is when deeper learning makes sense — reading documentation, experimenting deliberately, asking experienced people, reading books or blog posts. Teaching others is especially powerful: the moment you try to explain something, you discover the holes in your own understanding.

Learning never happens in order. You pick things up here and there — from tutorials, random videos, blog posts, or conversations. That’s completely normal.

When you catch yourself thinking “there must be a better way to do this”, mark it. Finish the prototype first. Then come back and go deep on that one topic.

Learning is not linear. But if you keep moving forward with a clear, end-to-end journey, the knowledge compounds quietly.

items.title: Frontend System Design Essentials is now ready
items.description: A practical path from everyday UI work to frontend system design
items.content:
As we step into the new year, I wanted to share a small but important update.

After several months of focused work — scripting, recording, editing, and refining — Frontend System Design Essentials is now complete and ready.

This course benefited a lot from early feedback and reviews. Many ideas were refined along the way, and I genuinely believe it’s stronger than what I originally planned. Thank you to everyone who shared thoughts and questions — they made a real difference.

The course is fully usable today, but it’s also a living resource. As more people go through it and discussions continue in the community, I’ll keep refining and improving it over time.

A thank-you for subscribers

As a thank-you to newsletter subscribers, you can get the course at a supporter price of $99 (the public price is $119).

Use this code at checkout: pragmatic2026

👉 Frontend System Design Essentials (course)
https://icodeit.thinkific.com/courses/frontend-system-design-essentials

Book vs course — how they work together

The book and the course are designed to complement each other, not repeat the same content.

They follow the same example and overall flow, but each format focuses on what it does best:

  • The course is more hands-on and visual, with code walkthroughs, refactoring sessions, and seeing the system evolve step by step.

  • The book goes deeper into code-level reasoning and design decisions — the kind of detailed explanation that would be slow or repetitive in video. It also includes written case studies, interview-focused material, and concrete frontend system design interview questions with worked-through solutions.

If you’d like to use both, there’s also a subscriber discount (pragmatic) for the book:

👉 Frontend System Design Essentials (book)
https://leanpub.com/frontend-system-design-essentials/c/pragmatic

There’s no pressure to buy — especially if now isn’t the right time. But if you’re actively thinking about frontend system design, interviews, or scaling real-world frontend applications, I hope these resources can be useful companions.

I’ll share a public announcement later this week, but I wanted you to hear about it here first. As always, feedback is very welcome.

Thanks again for your continued support,
Juntao

items.title: Wrapping Up the Year: A Few Thoughts and a Thank You
items.description: Frontend System Design, AI, and the Limits of Shortcuts
items.content:
It’s hard to believe that 2025 is almost over.

I know I said the same thing in last year’s end-of-year issue — probably the year before that as well — but it’s still true. Time feels like it’s moving faster every year.

Before anything else, thank you.

If you’re reading this — whether you’re a paid subscriber or on the free list — you’re supporting my work. And if something I wrote resonated with you, or helped you solve a real problem at work, that genuinely means a lot to me.

A quick recap of this year

This year, my main focus in content creation has been frontend system design.

I’ve been trying to teach the fundamental patterns and techniques that make frontend applications more performant, reliable, and maintainable — things like data modeling, pagination, caching, rendering strategies, and so on.

In some sense, this is a relatively new area for the industry.

The techniques themselves aren’t always new or fancy, but the systematic study of frontend systems is. Ten years ago, people were already trying to build fast web apps — but we didn’t really talk about “frontend system design” as a discipline.

The reason we can now is simple:

the browser ecosystem has matured, hardware has improved, and the web has become powerful enough that these concerns actually matter.

With new capabilities come new challenges.

This pattern isn’t new at all.

When new machines or techniques appear — whether it was the spinning jenny, higher-level programming languages, or now AI — work doesn’t disappear. It changes shape.

When C replaced assembly, we no longer had to think about registers and memory addresses all the time. But that didn’t mean programming became trivial. The complexity just moved to a different level of abstraction.

The same thing keeps happening, again and again.

New techniques enable new possibilities, which create new problems, which require new techniques. It’s a spiral, not a straight line — even in the AI era.

A few thoughts on AI

In many ways, AI feels similar to the shift from assembly to higher-level languages.

We may end up writing less code — that’s very likely.

But we’ll still need to understand systems, trade-offs, and constraints — just at a different abstraction level.

The details change, but the need for judgment doesn’t.

A personal observation

Recently, I’ve been experimenting with visualisation work — using Blender to render GIS data and generate map-like visuals.

This was a completely new area for me.

Materials, lighting, subdivisions, color ramps, planes, axes, shortcuts — everything felt unfamiliar.

What surprised me was how unhelpful AI felt in this situation.

When I work in areas I know well, AI can be genuinely useful. I can tell when it’s right, when it’s slightly off, and when it’s confidently wrong. Even subconsciously, I can sense what makes sense and what doesn’t.

But in a domain where I had no prior knowledge, that safety net disappeared.

AI could say things that sounded reasonable, but I had no way to judge them. I didn’t know whether the result was correct, accidental, or completely wrong. And because of that, I became impatient — not because AI was useless, but because I couldn’t trust myself to evaluate it.

That’s the tricky part.

AI works best when you already understand the problem space.

When you don’t, speed just gets you into trouble faster.

Why I’m still optimistic

Despite all this, I’m still optimistic about software engineering.

The work may look different. The tools will certainly change.

But the core skill — understanding how things actually work — still matters.

If you don’t understand what’s happening underneath, using AI will feel a lot like my early Blender experience: confusing, frustrating, and unreliable.

There are no real shortcuts here.

If it takes years to build judgment and intuition, it will probably still take years — even with better tools. The tools can help, but they don’t replace understanding.

That’s why I believe the kind of content I’m creating is still relevant.

When I teach, I always imagine a specific person: someone struggling with a hard topic, unsure what’s correct, and unsure who to trust. My goal is to show:

  • this is the idea

  • this is how we reason about it

  • this is how we verify it

  • and this is how you can apply it in your own work

Judgment is the real skill.

And if I can help you build that — even a little — then I’m doing my job.

A small update before the year ends

If you’ve been following my work on Frontend System Design Essentials, here’s a quick update.

  • The book is well underway, and you can already read the current version on Leanpub.

    If you’re interested, this link includes a coupon:

    https://leanpub.com/frontend-system-design-essentials/c/pragmatic

  • The course version is also very close.

    The content itself is done, and I’m now working through final touches — polishing lessons, tightening explanations, and making sure everything flows well. I’ll share more details very soon once it’s ready.

Thanks again for reading and for being here this year.

Have a great break, enjoy the end of the year, and I’ll see you in the next one.

The Pragmatic Developer is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

items.title: The Hidden Costs Behind Modern Frontend Complexity
items.description: A look back at how AJAX, browser standardisation, and the growing capabilities of the web quietly reshaped frontend development — and why each improvement also introduced a new layer of complexity we still manage today.
items.content:
In my talk about The Evolution of Frontend System Design, I touched on the hidden layers of complexity we face today — the complexity inside components, between components, and also across the whole system.

When John asked me on stage what the turning point was — the moment when everything suddenly became more complicated — my initial answer was that the complexity had always been there, mostly on the backend in the early 2000s. And I didn’t think there was a single moment where things “got out of hand.”

But after the talk, when I thought about it more carefully, I realised there was a moment that opened the door, almost like Pandora’s box. And that moment was AJAX — a technique that lets the browser fetch data in the background and update only part of the page, without doing a full-page reload. On the surface, it was a strange name and easy to misunderstand. But it was a fundamental turning point that changed how we interact with the web.

Think about how search used to work. There was a search box and a big “Search” button next to it. The user typed their query, clicked the button, and the entire page refreshed with new results. The URL changed, pagination info appeared, and everything was a full-page reload.

When AJAX arrived, that changed. Suddenly we didn’t need a “submit” button. We could search as the user typed. The list below updated instantly, without refreshing the whole page.

It was a huge improvement in user experience. Most of the page stayed the same, and only a small part changed.

But the moment we unlocked that experience, we also unlocked new responsibilities:

we now had to cancel previous in-flight requests, debounce keystrokes so we didn’t trigger a search on every character, and cache previous queries to avoid overwhelming the server.

This is the cost we pay for better user experience — and this is only one example.

Another turning point, as I mentioned on stage, was the release of Chrome and the gradual unification of browsers. Frontend developers used to spend an incredible amount of time dealing with browser differences. Some features didn’t work well in IE, Firefox behaved differently, and Safari had its own logic for certain events. We spent a lot of energy solving these inconsistencies instead of focusing on the important abstractions.

When browsers finally became more standardised, and as Web APIs improved, the frontend suddenly had much more power. We gained local storage, better debugging tools, access to cameras and microphones, and more reliable APIs. With a more stable foundation, we started building richer and more interactive applications.

As the platform matured — faster JavaScript engines, better networking, and a more consistent CSS model — the frontend became capable of things simply not possible before. And once those capabilities arrived, more complexity naturally followed. Richer experiences meant more state to manage, more edge cases, and more logic that used to live only on the backend.

And this pattern continues even today. We now have to think about client-side routing, accessibility, storage, state management, security concerns, code splitting, lazy loading, and the supporting techniques — testing strategies, bundling, image optimisation, and so on.

And that’s just today.

As the platform keeps evolving, what we can do tomorrow will almost certainly go beyond what we can do now. AI is a perfect example. It will likely change many interactions we consider “standard” today and reshape how we design for the web. The old model of interacting with the UI through precise commands — clicking a button, pressing a key — may become less relevant when you can simply tell the system what you want.

That future is already visible in tools like ChatGPT — and it leaves an open question: how should we design for a world where intent becomes the primary interface?

By the way, my Frontend System Design Essentials course is almost finished. I’ve been putting the final touches on it, and I’m excited to share it soon. If this topic interests you, feel free to join the waitlist. I’ll share an update with everyone on the list as soon as the course goes live.

items.title: Why Simple Frontend Tasks Become Complicated
items.description: A practical look at modern frontend complexity — and the frameworks that help us manage it.
items.content:
(A refined write-up of my talk “The Evolution of Frontend System Design” from Web Directions 2025.)

Earlier this week, I gave a talk at Web Directions titled “The Evolution of Frontend System Design.” It was a fun session to deliver, and many people told me afterwards that the story about the “simple dropdown” really resonated with them. Quite a few developers said something along the lines of: “Yep... that’s exactly what happens to me at work.”

So in this issue, I want to share a clean written version of that talk — not just a transcript, but a slightly expanded version. I’ve refined some of the explanations, added a couple of frameworks I didn’t have time to cover on stage, and included a clearer structure so you can use this as a reference for your own work.

If you were at the talk, this will feel familiar. If you weren’t, think of this as the full story — with all the parts I would have added if I had more time on the conference clock.

Let’s dive in.

A “Simple” Component That Isn’t Actually Simple

Imagine you’re asked to build a user-select component. It starts off collapsed, and when you click it, it expands and shows a list of users. You’ve seen this pattern in many tools — Jira, Trello, GitHub Issues, Notion.

The first version looks easy. Fetch the users, store them in state, show the list. Add a loading state, add an error state — still simple.

But complexity shows up quickly in real products.

A customer with two thousand employees reports that opening the dropdown freezes the app. Rendering everything at once isn’t viable anymore. So you add pagination. And suddenly, you’re coordinating with backend changes, versioning concerns, or feature switches.

Then they ask for search. And search brings its own layers: debouncing, request cancellation, empty states, error handling, and combining search with pagination.

Accessibility arrives next. Keyboard navigation doesn’t work. Screen readers don’t announce highlighted items. Now you’re adding ARIA attributes, focus management, and testing interactions you previously didn’t think much about.

Then localisation. Some languages overflow the UI. Some wrap differently. Arabic needs right-to-left layout. Now your styles and layout logic evolve too.

And when you step back, you realise this “simple” dropdown now has loading logic, error boundaries, virtualisation, debounced search, pagination, accessibility layers, RTL support, and multiple network states.

Still “just a dropdown,” but in reality, a system of behaviours and constraints.

This is what I covered in the first half of the talk. And it illustrates the first kind of complexity we face in frontend — internal component complexity. Real requirements turn the happy path into something much deeper.

But that’s not the whole story.

A Small UI Change Can Trigger System-Wide Behaviour

Let’s zoom out.

Assigning a user to a card doesn’t just update the little avatar. It affects the card itself, the board view, the sorting logic, the sidebar counters, the activity feed, and any real-time views other people have open.

So the questions shift from UI to system coordination.

Should we update optimistically?

What if the server rejects the update?

What if someone else assigns the card at the same time?

How do we keep caches consistent across different parts of the UI?

This is the second type of complexity — system-level complexity.

None of these problems belong to the dropdown component. They belong to the entire application. And they show up in every serious frontend project.

In the talk, I compared this to backend system design. When multiple parts of a system need to coordinate, architecture and boundaries start to matter. The same applies in frontend today.

A Simple Lifecycle Framework to Make Sense of Frontend

This is where I paused my talk on Wednesday and introduced a simple model to help people organise their thinking. I want to expand it here because it’s been one of the most helpful ways for me to understand frontend work over the years.

You can think of frontend through the lens of the application lifecycle:

Build time → Deployment time → Runtime

This framework helps you place each challenge in the right mental drawer.

Build time is where your code is transformed.

Bundlers, code splitting, SSR choices, static generation, types, tests — all the things that shape your application before it reaches users.

Deployment time is how your code gets delivered.

CDNs, caching, routing, compression, asset invalidation — the infrastructure that controls performance and reliability.

Runtime is where things get interesting. This is when the browser runs your code and users start interacting with it. And this is where most of the complexity appears — data fetching, caching, rendering, accessibility, localisation, concurrency, real-time updates, and error handling.

When a frontend task suddenly grows, it’s often because it spans multiple stages of this lifecycle. A UI change that seems purely visual may have runtime implications, caching implications, and deployment implications all at once.

This simple model doesn’t solve your problems, but it gives you a map — and a map reduces confusion.

The Framework I Use for Frontend System Design

During the talk, I briefly mentioned my Frontend System Design Essentials series. Here, I want to give you a clearer sense of the framework behind it, because this is how I personally break down complex frontend features.

At runtime, almost everything falls into a few predictable categories:

  • How you fetch data

  • How you model data

  • How you store and manage state

  • How you mutate data and handle conflicts or errors

  • How you render in a way that balances performance and clarity

  • How you handle cross-cutting behaviours like i18n, accessibility, real-time events, and loading strategies

Most frontend challenges can be explained by understanding which of these areas are involved, and how they interact.

When something feels unexpectedly complex, the framework helps you see why. And when you design a new feature, it helps you identify dependencies and risk earlier.

This is the part I didn’t have enough time to expand on during the talk, so I’m glad I get to share it here.

Closing Thoughts

Frontend used to be about manipulating the DOM. Today, it’s about building and coordinating multiple subsystems running inside the browser. That’s why tasks feel bigger than they initially look.

But with the right mental models, this complexity becomes manageable. You understand why features expand. You see how small UI pieces connect to larger systems. And you can diagnose problems more confidently because you know where they live.

If you were at the Web Directions talk, I hope this extended write-up gives you a deeper and more structured view of the material. If you weren’t, I hope it offers you a useful way to think about frontend work in your own projects.

items.title: The Joy of Typing to Your Tools
items.description: Discover how the evolution from the Linux terminal to tools like IntelliJ, Slack, and ChatGPT reflects a timeless truth: the best interfaces let us think through our keyboards. This essay explores the enduring joy of typing to your tools and the future of seamless, conversational computing.
items.content:
When I first started programming, I discovered the Linux system—Fedora Core 4, I believe—and soon after, I was captivated by the command line. It felt magical to interact with the computer using just a keyboard, without a proper GUI. You could read documentation, browse files, send emails, play music, and most importantly, write code—all without touching a mouse. That just felt cool.

Later, when I moved to Windows, I often found myself wondering: Where can I just type what I want and have the computer respond?

I didn’t want to dig through menus or click through toolbars—I just wanted to type, or even speak what I was thinking, and have the computer get it done. Even today, I still get frustrated by poor UI—mostly on some websites, since I haven’t used Windows in a long time.

Keyboard shortcuts helped, for sure. With enough of them, I could still be fast and efficient. But they came with a cost: I had to remember a lot of them—especially when switching between tools. As a Java developer, I mainly used Eclipse and NetBeans, but their keyboard shortcuts weren’t great, particularly for auto-refactorings like extracting a block into a function, moving a function to another file, or running tests for the current Java class.

Then I was introduced to IntelliJ, the default IDE at Thoughtworks. It didn’t take long to realize how powerful it was. And, as you might guess, my favorite shortcut quickly became Shift + Cmd + A.

This one shortcut lets you search for any action by name—no more digging through menus. Just type what you want and run it.

Read more

items.title: Carving Beyond “Good Enough”
items.description: What carving a simple wooden gourd taught me about creativity, fear, and finding the courage to go one cut further — in work and in life.
items.content:
I’ve been learning wood carving recently, and I found it to be an incredibly addictive hobby. There’s something deeply satisfying about guiding the knife through the wood, following the grain, and watching a shape slowly emerge at your fingertips.

It’s easy to fall into the zone — hours can pass by without noticing. Your hands and arms grow tired, but while you’re in it, you barely feel the fatigue or your surroundings. You have a clear goal (you usually know what you’re carving), instant feedback, and a simple next step — another cut.

That’s probably one of the easiest ways to enter a flow state: when your full attention is dedicated to something, and the whole power of yourself — your focus, patience, and creativity — starts to reveal itself.

But what I found even more interesting is the bravery that carving requires — the courage to step outside your comfort zone, even when it feels intimidating.

For example, I was carving a Hulu (葫芦) — a traditional Chinese gourd

often seen as a symbol of luck (Hu) and wealth (Lu). You’ve probably seen it in Chinese movies; it’s sometimes used as a container. The shape is basically two connected spheres — the smaller one on top, the larger one below. It’s not hard to carve, but the challenge lies in getting the proportions right.

After about an hour, my first attempt looked okay — the two spheres were about the same size. But as I refined the details, I started to worry:

What if my next cut ruins everything?

That’s a familiar fear. I’ve felt it in coding too. Sometimes, when I have a version that’s “good enough,” I stop — even when I know I could make it better. I hesitate, afraid of making things worse or losing control of what already works.

It’s a reasonable instinct — caution protects our progress. But I’ve also realized it’s something I could improve. At some point, I told myself:

It’s just a block of wood. If I mess it up, I can start again.

So I did. The first few cuts were nerve-wracking, but I kept going. Bit by bit, confidence returned, and thirty minutes later, I had a much better version of the Hulu.

Sometimes, “good enough” is what keeps us tied to mediocrity. It’s hard to break away from that safe zone. But growth often comes from those moments when we choose to take the risk — to push the boundary a little further, even when everything seems fine as it is.

Thanks for reading The Pragmatic Developer! This post is public so feel free to share it.

Share

items.title: Writing as a Tool for Thinking
items.description: We often treat writing as a communication skill, but for developers, it’s also a learning tool. This piece explores how writing helps you clarify complex ideas, connect concepts, and turn experience into deep understanding.
items.content:
I write a lot — from daily records of things I’ve tried and learned, to weekly work logs, from internal docs explaining how to complete certain tasks, to deeper reflections intended for a broader audience.

Most of the time, though, I write for myself. Many of these notes will probably never be published. I used to think of it simply as a good habit, but over time I realized it’s actually one of the best ways to learn. Whenever I decide to write about something, I find myself understanding it more deeply. I learn the vocabulary, the concepts, how they connect, the subtle nuances between them, and how new ideas relate to what I already know.

At some point, I watched a lecture where the professor asked the class, “What is the purpose of writing?” He explained that people often use writing as a tool for thinking. That idea struck me deeply. I started to see writing not just as a way to share knowledge, but as a tool to untangle thoughts hidden beneath first-hand experiences.

For example, I once learned how to use Chrome DevTools for performance measurement. That led me to resources about Web Vitals, which then introduced me to local performance metrics, field data, and tools that let me measure performance programmatically with the Profiler. From there, I discovered ways to integrate performance tests into continuous integration pipelines — using command-line tools to generate automated reports.

These are all first-hand experiences, but if you don’t capture them, they fade within weeks. However, if you take the time to structure your thoughts, connect them to existing knowledge, and have an inner dialogue while writing — or even while resting or taking a shower — those experiences begin to solidify. They sink in, forming something you can draw upon later. In a sense, they become part of your internal knowledge base.

As someone who has been in the field for a while, I often get asked for advice. Unsurprisingly, I always encourage people to write. Even if you never share your writing publicly, it’s an incredible tool to shape your thoughts. When you write, your ideas take form — and as you express them, they become clearer and more organized.

Many of my books actually started this way. They didn’t begin as books. I would have a rough idea about a topic, do some research, create a mind map to structure my thoughts, and then fill in the gaps. Over time, this process naturally formed an outline. Some parts were unclear at first — even unknown — but through writing, I gradually discovered and built the entire structure of the book. Writing was the process of creation and discovery.

I also find that simply writing book notes helps immensely — especially when I want to remember what I’ve just read, or review it the next day. Our memory works in a way that the harder it is to retrieve information, the better it sticks in the long term. Rewriting or rephrasing what you’ve read, listened to, or watched — whether it’s a book, podcast, or YouTube video — dramatically improves understanding and retention.

Like many things that compound, the more knowledge you have in your “mental database,” the easier it becomes to organize and connect the dots — forming new, more complex ideas.

So start writing — even if it’s just to refine what you learned today, to jot down a small idea, or to capture a random thought that just came to you. Over time, you’ll find your thinking becomes sharper, your insights deeper, and your understanding much stronger.

The Pragmatic Developer is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

items.title: There’s No real-world Standard
items.description: Make deliberate, context-driven decisions—define constraints, weigh trade-offs, and resist copy-pasting big-tech patterns
items.content:
Many readers ask for more real-world projects and “how big tech does it.” I get it. We all want patterns that work and examples we can trust. Here’s the counter-intuitive truth I’ve learned: there isn’t a single real-world standard.

Background & Design Challenge

In my first decade as a frontend engineer, I worked across 20+ projects—tiny single-page sites, ad platforms, and large product codebases. The tech stacks overlapped, the team shapes looked similar, and the problem domains sometimes rhymed. But the projects? Never the same.

Even in a narrow slice like the frontend build, the details vary. One team glued together RequireJS with a Jenkins shell script to bundle and deploy. Another built a Webpack pipeline with multiple environments, release gates, and automated rollbacks. At a travel client, static bundles went through Akamai with custom purge scripts, while at a fintech client, we shipped to S3 behind CloudFront with fine-grained cache-control rules.

So the design challenge isn’t “What’s the standard?” It’s “How do I make solid decisions when there are no universal templates?”

Think of building an online ordering system for pizza, or a ticket booking system, a coffee shop with a subscription for beans, or even a government application system. They are all production applications— real-world systems—but their requirements, especially the non-functional ones, are very different. Some systems naturally demand near real-time updates, like chat apps or ticket booking. Others don’t. A few days of delay on coffee bean delivery doesn’t carry the same weight as a failed banking transaction or visa application. What counts as real-world depends heavily on the type of system you’re building.

It’s also easy to assume that large applications, especially giant monorepos packed with features, are what makes something “real world.” But even in those environments, you’ll only ever touch a small slice of the codebase. In big organizations, you still work in relatively small feature teams—maybe ten engineers or so—focused on a single surface. Take Jira’s board view as an example. A team there spends its time on feature work, bug fixes, operations, security, accessibility, and adopting new platform libraries. Day to day, that doesn’t feel dramatically different from a small product team in a smaller company.

Of course, scale does bring real challenges. Codebases change rapidly, and many teams contribute at once. But that doesn’t mean you need to understand every corner. Treat other teams’ work almost like external dependencies. You know React exists, you know TypeScript evolves, but you don’t dive into their internals to use them productively. The same applies when you share a repo with dozens of other teams: you need awareness, not mastery of every detail.

That’s also why copying big tech can be risky. At Atlassian, for example, we lean on Relay and GraphQL. They serve us well because we have a central schema team managing global types, internal tooling to surface schema changes, entrypoints that preload both code and data, and dedicated infra teams monitoring performance. If you adopt GraphQL and Relay without that support, you inherit the complexity but not the safety net. What looks like a best practice in one company may become a burden in another.

The same thing happened with microservices. A few years back, they were the default answer to everything. I saw small teams with five engineers split into a dozen services. They spent more time patching CI pipelines and debugging network timeouts than shipping features. A well-structured monolith would have been faster and easier. It’s a reminder that scaling patterns down doesn’t always work.

And even when a pattern is genuinely useful, it always comes with trade-offs. Take normalizing client data. On the plus side, updates become simpler, UI stays consistent, and cache invalidation is easier. In our Trello-like app, moving a card only updates one record, and every list reflects the change instantly. But rendering nested views requires extra hydration work. Assembling a board view means fetching columns, then stitching cards by ID. Without careful selectors, this adds complexity and potential performance overhead.

So what’s the takeaway? Don’t start with the tool. Start with the problem. Write down the constraints and success criteria before choosing a solution. Then weigh the trade-offs: what do you gain, what do you pay, and who bears the cost—users, infrastructure, or developers? If you try a new tool, time-box the experiment and adopt it incrementally so you can attribute wins (or pain) to a specific change. And most importantly, don’t chase FOMO. You’re not behind if you haven’t shipped microfrontends or jumped on the latest trend.

Learn broadly. Choose deliberately. Don’t copy an architecture because a big company uses it; understand why it works for them and whether those conditions exist for you. There’s no universal template—only trade-offs to weigh in your context. That’s the real real world.

items.title: Designing Frontend Systems Starts with Data Modeling
items.description: Data modeling isn’t just for the backend. In this issue, we’ll explore how understanding the domain and shaping data around UI access patterns is the foundation of scalable frontend systems.
items.content:
Opening

When we first learn frontend development, data modelling doesn’t feel that important.

You grab some JSON, put it into state, and render it. For a small demo app, that’s often enough.

But as soon as your application grows, cracks begin to show:

  • You start duplicating state.

  • Updating one part of the UI doesn’t update another.

  • Or you realize you can’t easily represent a feature the product manager just asked for.

That’s when it clicks: data modeling is not just a backend concern — it’s fundamental to frontend architecture too.

When the Domain Surprises You (Chat App)

Take a chat app. At first, it looks simple:

  • A User.

  • A Message.

  • Maybe an Attachment.

But once you consider real-world scenarios, more concepts appear:

  • Threads that group conversations.

  • Private groups with restricted membership.

  • Broadcast messages that can’t be replied to.

  • Archived messages that change how history is displayed.

None of these were obvious in the first JSON response. You only discover them by digging into the domain rules.

When Simple Models Aren’t Enough (Course Platform)

Or think about an online course platform.

The first pass at modeling might be: Course, Module, Lesson. Plus Student and Instructor.

But that barely scratches the surface.

Read more

items.title: 🚀 Presale Now Open: Frontend System Design Essentials
items.description: Join the first 32 and help shape Frontend System Design Essentials.
items.content:
I’ve opened presale for my new course, Frontend System Design Essentials.

  • Regular price is $119

  • Presale price for subscribers: $79

  • Only 32 seats total (7 already taken)

  • If we don’t reach 32, I’ll simply refund everyone

👉 Reserve your seat here (get your discount with coupon PRAGMATIC in checkout)

Why I’m Doing This

Over the past months, I’ve shared a few videos on frontend system design — things like state management, data fetching, pagination, and optimistic UI.

They got a lot more traction than I expected. More importantly, I received really thoughtful feedback and questions from many of you.

That made me realize: there aren’t many good resources that go deeper into frontend system design. Most material out there is about backend architecture, or it only scratches the surface on frontend.

So this course is my attempt to fill that gap.

Why a Presale?

I want to build this together with an initial group of learners — people who care about the same problems I do.

  • If enough of you (32) join, I’ll proceed and finish building the full course.

  • If not, I’ll refund everyone, no risk involved.

  • And since you’re reading this here, you get the lowest public price: $79 (instead of $119).

👉 Join the presale (Get your discount with coupon PRAGMATIC at checkout.)

What You’ll Learn

This isn’t about just making components work. It’s about learning to think like a frontend architect — someone who can design apps that scale, stay reliable, and perform well in the real world.

The course will cover the following essential areas:

  1. The clear roadmap for learning Frontend System Design

  2. Data modelling & state management in depth

  3. Data fetching & caching

  4. Data mutation & real-time updates

  5. Rendering strategies (CSR, SSR, SSG, streaming)

  6. Performance optimization

  7. Infrastructure & cross-functional needs (accessibility, error handling, CI/CD)

And you’ll tie it all together in a capstone project: a Trello-like app that’s production-ready and resilient.

Why Now

With AI writing more and more of the boilerplate code, what makes a developer valuable isn’t just knowing syntax or APIs — it’s being able to design systems.

That’s what this course is about: helping you build the mindset and techniques to think at that level.

Only 32 seats in total — once we hit that number, the presale will close immediately.
Joining now means you’ll not only secure the lowest price, but also help shape the course as it’s being built.

👉 Reserve your presale seat (Get your discount with coupon PRAGMATIC at checkout.)

See you inside,

Juntao Qiu

items.title: This Week In React #285: React.foundation, Rust Compiler, Sätteri, Motion, TanStack Table, React Router, Flow, NavLink | Runtimes, JSI, Standard Navigation, Testing Library, Static Hermes, BottomTabs, AGP, AI, Windows | VoidZero, npm, Rolldown, Angular
items.description: Hi everyone, Seb and Jan here 👋\!
items.content:
Hi everyone, Seb and Jan here 👋!

This week, we’re taking a look at the new React Foundation website, as the React core repositories transition to their new home. All eyes on the React Compiler in Rust, coming soon in your everyday toolchain.

React Native 0.86 is almost here, but releases apparently like to land on Wednesday evenings 😅 so we’ll cover it next week. Let’s focus on React Native Runtimes instead, a quite interesting multi-threading innovation.

Cloudflare has acquired VoidZero, and npm v12 will officially block postinstall scripts by default in July, improving our supply chain security.

Let's dive in!

As always, thanks for supporting us on your favorite platform:

  • 🦋 Bluesky
  • ✖️ X / Twitter
  • 👔 LinkedIn
  • 👽 Reddit

Don't miss the next email!

    💸 Sponsor​

    Ship AI generated code safely with Meticulous.

    Claude writes your code. Claude reviews your code. Claude fixes the review comments. And somehow, you're the one getting paged at 2am when it breaks in prod.

    Fortunately, top AI-driven teams like Dropbox, Notion, LaunchDarkly, and Wiz rely on Meticulous to run 1000s of e2e UI tests autonomously, covering every user flow, edge case, role and permutation. Built by ex-Palantir engineers, Meticulous gives you near-exhaustive coverage in weeks, without any developer effort.

    It works like magic in the background:

    • Near-exhaustive coverage on every test run
    • No test creation
    • No maintenance (seriously)
    • Zero flakes (built on a deterministic browser)

    Check it out - and see why one engineering leader at Dropbox said that “once we started using Meticulous, we couldn’t imagine working without it.”

    ⚛️ React​

    The React.Foundation website

    The React Foundation was announced nearly a year ago, and you may be wondering what actions it has taken so far. Did you know the React Foundation had a website? Although it’s still a work-in-progress (source code), it’s already up and running and worth taking a look at to discover what’s planned, such as:

    • A system to fund maintainers of critical ecosystem libraries
    • An official React merchandise store with profits redistributed to maintainers
    • Quarterly transparent reports with revenue details, maintainer funding, and more
    • A logged-in “Contributor Status” page, adding a cool gamification layer

    Earlier today, most React-related repositories also moved from the facebook GitHub organization to the react GitHub organization, reflecting the transition to the React Foundation. This includes React, React Native, Yoga, JSX, Metro, React-Strict-DOM, Create-React-App, and more.

    It’s not facebook/react anymore, but react/react: end of an era!

    • 💸 PlanetScale - Low-latency queries, branch and deploy like code, and best-in-class reliability. All in one database.
    • 👀 React Core PR - Port React Compiler to Rust - The PR has been officially merged, although no Rust crate or npm package has been published yet. The Oxc integration is available in Rust crate v0.135, and it’s being integrated in Rolldown and in Oxlint. See also the SWC integration PR and the Bun integration PR.
    • 👀 React RFC - Support using disposable as useEffect cleanup - A proposal for useEffect to integrate with the new ES feature Explicit Resource Management / using, allowing to return a disposable resource like {[Symbol.dispose]: myEffectCleanupFunction}.
    • 🗓️ React Advanced London - 🇬🇧 London - 23 & 26 Oct. Call for Papers is open until June 22! Submit your talk and share your advanced React insights with the community. Get a 10% discount with code "TWIR".
    • 📜 Flow for TypeScript Users in 2026 - Flow’s syntax has converged with TypeScript to the point where it’s hard to tell them apart anymore. It’s stricter and offers features TypeScript lacks, including pattern matching and component/hook/renders syntax. I don’t know if Flow is ready for an official comeback, but I like its direction. Note that its compiler is also being ported to Rust,
    • 📜 Building an Active NavLink Component in Next.js - Building a production-grade reusable NavLink component for the App Router with an isActive/className render prop API inspired by React Router. It’s surprisingly not so easy: you need to deal with initial paint flickering and need to use Suspense for Cache Components.
    • 📜 The Best Loading States Are No Loading States - Route transitions, route loaders, preloading, and global fallbacks can make loading states mostly disappear.
    • 📜 How React Server Components Integrate with Bundler
    • 📜 When React parent components need to know their children
    • 📜 The problem with useEffect – React Doctor can help
    • 💸 Certificates.dev: Certification AMA Workshop. Ask our expert how exams are built, graded, proctored, and impacts careers. Join for free!
    • 📦 Sätteri - Flexible Rust-based Markdown / MDX engine - A faster Rust-based alternative to the Unified ecosystem and the JS-based implementation of MDX. It could become Astro’s default parser in the future, and already integrates with Starlight.
    • 📦 Motion 12.40 - Support for arc() motions - This lets you animate elements along curved paths.
    • 📦 TanStack Table 9.0 beta - State management and perf improvements, tree-shakable, extendable, composable - TanStack Table took inspiration from TanStack Form state management to rebuild its state management on top of TanStack Store and atoms, preventing useless re-renders and becoming compatible with the React Compiler along the way.
    • 📦 React Router 8.0 prerelease - The next major version is now available for early adopters to try. It’s a relatively boring release, in a good way: the v8 future flags are gone, and their behaviors are now enabled by default. It also bumps runtime requirements, drops CJS and the react-router-dom package.
    • 📦 React Router 7.17 - Ship Markdown docs for AI agents
    • 📦 React 19.2.7, 19.1.8, 19.0.7 - Fix a Server Actions FormData regression
    • 🔗 This Month in React - Who even is on the Core team anymore, TanStack got pwn'd bad
    • 🔗 Señors at Scale - Routing at Scale with TanStack Router's Nicolas Beaussart

    Don't miss the next email!

      💸 Sponsor​

      Agent Conf 2026: Learn to Ship Cross-Platform with AI

      Callstack’s Agent Conf (September 17-18, 2026, Warsaw) is bringing together hundreds of agentic engineering practitioners, with Nader Dabit, Levan Kvirkvelia, Tejas Kumar, Kent C. Dodds, Kitze, David Mokos, and more speakers to be announced soon. Learn how to work better with agents: build intuition for when to guide or let them run, understand loops, memory, cost, and failure modes, coordinate multi-agent systems, and hear what’s actually working for teams adopting agentic workflows today.

      The last Early Bird Tickets are still available. Get an extra 10% off with promo code TWIR.

      📱 React-Native​

      React Native Runtimes

      Margelo, in collaboration with Callstack, introduced a new package to add a multi-runtime layer to React Native apps. It’s a great solution for performance-heavy features and business logic isolation to avoid blocking interaction.

      If your app has features that jank on mount, slow first-open screens, background hydration or decoding, state logic competing with animation, it might be worth checking it out.

      Key features:

      • Run selected components, screens, and headless tasks.
      • Share state across runtimes through a native Zustand-style C++ singleton.
      • Prewarm runtimes to avoid cold-start lag.
      • Call a typed function on a specific runtime and await the result.
      • Expo support with a config plugin.
      • 💸 Product for Engineers - The golden rules of agent-first product engineering
      • 👀 React Native RFC - Android Gradle Plugin v9 adoption - This drafts a three-phase AGP v9 adoption strategy to keep up with Android, doing the required changes to support built-in Kotlin before the opt-outs are removed in AGP v10.
      • 👀 Static Hermes - What's new in the next stable release - This blog post announces many new ES features to be supported natively, including Set operations, Iterator helpers, groupBy, TextDecoder, and more. It will also be faster and support built-in TypeScript type-stripping!
      • 👀 Standard Navigation integration PRs - Remember Expo Router 56 forking React Navigation? Fortunately, we have a shared abstraction for creating navigators that can work with both routing libraries, and should help reduce ecosystem fragmentation. It has already been integrated into React Navigation (PR) and Expo Router (PR).
      • 🗓️ Chain React - 🇺🇸 Portland - 30-31 July. Incredible speaker lineup featuring Charlie Cheever (Expo), Seth Webster (React Foundation), Kacper Kapuściak (Software Mansion), Mazen Chami (Infinite Red) and more. Get a 15% discount with code "TWIR".
      • 🗓️ React Native Connection - 🇫🇷 Paris - 24 Sept. The premier French React Native conference is back! Secure your spot early and get a 15% discount with code "TWIR". Call for Papers closes June 14.
      • 📅 React Native & AI meet-up by Software Mansion - June 18th, NYC 🇺🇸
      • 📜 Talking to JSI in Swift: what changed in SDK 56 - In SDK 56, Expo's native modules call JSI directly on Apple platforms. The Objective-C++ layer is gone, and calls are 1.6–2.3x faster.
      • 📜 Moving away from @expo/vector-icons - Explanation on why the icons package is deprecated in favor of a better alternative, and the benefits of switching.
      • 📜 Exploring Inlined Requires - A 3-part series of Metro’s inlineRequires: the transform mechanics, when flipping the flag doesn’t improve cold start, and how Expo Router solves it with lazy loading.
      • 📜 On-device AI after WWDC 2026: What's new? - A look into new AI features from Apple and how React Native will make use of them.
      • 📜 AI-Supported Brownfield Migration to React Native
      • 📜 What happened at AppJS 2026? Highlights, new products, and plans for the future
      • 📦 React Native Testing Library 14.0 - React 19, async APIs, Test Renderer replacement
      • 📦 React Native Windows 0.83 - Performance Testing Framework, Richer Pointer Event Support, Fabric Component Parity
      • 📦 React Native DocuSign - Native captive signing for React Native & Expo
      • 📦 Lynx 3.8 - WebAssembly on Android, Adaptive Layout, Line-aware Auto Font Sizing, monthly release cadence
      • 📦 Livechart - High-performance live line & candlestick charts for React Native, built on Skia and Reanimated
      • 📦 Nitro Fetch 1.4 - Support for local and non-HTTP URLs
      • 📦 Data Scanner - Fast one-shot QR/Barcode scanning, powered by Nitro and native scanner UIs
      • 📦 Agent Device 0.17 - iOS runner stability, perf metrics, replay test sharding
      • 📦 Rozenite 1.12 - DevTools middleware fixes, compose sections across hook instances, support Expo bundle asset listings
      • 📦 Bottom Tabs 1.3 - Liquid Glass tint color improvements
      • 🎥 Simon Grimm - I used Expo and Vega OS to make a Fire TV App
      • 🎥 Daniel Friyia Jr - Intro to 3D Graphics with WebGPU and Expo

      🔀 Other​

      • 📣 VoidZero is Joining Cloudflare - Cloudflare acquired the company behind Vite, Vitest, Rolldown, Oxc, Oxfmt, Oxlint, and Vite+. These tools will remain open-source, MIT-licensed, and community-driven. Cloudflare promises no lock-in and plans to build its new cf CLI around Vite.
      • 📣 GitHub - Upcoming breaking changes for npm v12 - GitHub announces that npm v12 (scheduled for July) will improve security-related default behaviors, blocking install lifecycle scripts by default!
      • 📜 Why does tsgo use so much memory? - It runs one type checker per thread.
      • 📦 Rolldown 1.1 - Enables lazy barrel optimization, aligns TS references resolution with tsc
      • 📦 Angular 22.0 - Signal Forms, Angular Aria, OnPush-by-default, async DI, IA, and more

      🤭 Fun​

      See ya! 👋

items.title: This Week In React #284: TanStack Start, Compiler, React Router, Forms, TypeGPU, React Aria, shadcn, XState | App.js, Gesture Handler, SPM, Expo, Runtimes, Legend List, Desktop | npm, Node.js, Astro
items.description: Hi everyone, Kacper and Filip from Software Mansion here.
items.content:
Hi everyone, Kacper and Filip from Software Mansion here.

This week, TanStack Start is once again in the spotlight. The React Compiler in Rust is on its way. React Router and Remix shipped important security patches – update immediately. There's also a fresh batch of releases from TanStack Form, XState Store, shadcn, React Aria, and more.

On the React Native side, this week was dominated by App.js Conf 2026 in Kraków. Gesture Handler 3.0, Swift Package Manager support for React Native, and Legend List 3.0 were among the highlights, alongside Expo announcements like EAS Observe.

Let's go!

As always, thanks for supporting us on your favorite platform:

  • 🦋 Bluesky
  • ✖️ X / Twitter
  • 👔 LinkedIn
  • 👽 Reddit

Don't miss the next email!

    💸 Sponsor​

    Atomic CRM: The Open-Source CRM Toolkit for Developers

    Stop struggling with locked-in CRMs and expensive seats. Atomic CRM gives you the power of a professional CRM with the total freedom of open-source.

    It’s the only toolkit that combines a high-end user experience with data sovereignty. No more lock-in, no more "renting" your contacts.

    Everything you need is already there:

    • Native Mobile App for on-the-go access.
    • Intuitive Kanban Boards for pipeline management.
    • Built-in Email Tracking to stay on top of leads.
    • Free SSO for seamless team integration.
    • MCP Server Integration for productivity gains.

    Why settle for a black box SaaS when you can own the entire platform? Deploy Atomic CRM on your own infrastructure in minutes and regain control over your most valuable asset: your data.

    ⚛️ React​

    TanStack Start Gaining Momentum:

    • 📜 TanStack Start Adds First-Class Rsbuild Support - TanStack Start now supports Rsbuild / Rspack alongside Vite via a new plugin adapter, covering SSR, streaming, HMR, Server Functions, and RSC.
    • 📜 Lovable - Building apps using TanStack Start - The AI App builder is now using TanStack Start with SSR by default for all new projects.
    • 📜 The Conductor Rewrite: What They Changed to Make It Fast - Migrating their Tauri desktop app from React Router to TanStack Router significantly reduced re-renders thanks to its built-in structural sharing and stable refs.
    • 📜 TanStack Start authentication: A developer's guide for 2026 - Why beforeLoad doesn't protect server functions, the double-guard pattern, sessions, and middleware.
    • 💸 SVAR React Gantt – Build project timelines with a pure React API and advanced scheduling (MIT & PRO)
    • 👀 React Compiler update - The Rust compiler PR to be merged soon - It already produces at 99.9% the same output as the original TS compiler. You can try it already through a local PR checkout. The Oxc team is already working on an integration (PR).
    • 📅 Next.js Nights - The Next.js team on tour (9 June SF, 11 June Amsterdam, 18 June London) - They also teased us about an upcoming v16.3 release, including Instant Navigations and new AI-related features.
    • 🔐 React Router & Remix v2 - 7 CVEs published - Includes RCE, CSRF, XSS, and DoS vulnerabilities. Update to React Router v7.16.0 / v6.30.4 or Remix v2.17.5.
    • 📜 Deprecating a React component using TypeScript Overload - How to ship a breaking component redesign without renaming it with the usage of TS overload, mark the old API as @deprecated, and let consumers opt into the new version by adding a new prop.
    • 💸 PostHog - PostHog Code and the self-driving product
    • 📦 TanStack Form 1.33 - FormGroup API to simplify multi-step forms
    • 📦 React Hook Form 7.77 - new resetDefaultValues API
    • 📦 React Email 6.5 - Filter compatibility warnings by client, accessibility improvements
    • 📦 TypeGPU React - React bindings for TypeGPU - Hooks like useFrame, useUniform, and useRoot to write GPU shaders directly in TypeScript and integrate WebGPU into React components.
    • 📦 Lexical 0.45 - New composable DOMImportExtension replacing importDOM, backspace now preserves block type, canUndo/canRedo signals on HistoryExtension.
    • 📦 React Aria Components 1.18 - Calendar multi-date selection, month/year pickers, new TableFooter and SliderFill components - Checkbox, Radio, and Switch now support description and error message slots.
    • 📦 React Spectrum 1.4 - drag & drop in ListView and similar, TableView highlight selection & TableFooter, custom prefixes in ComboBox/TextField, multi-date Calendar selection
    • 📦 Shadcn UI 4.9 & 4.10 - GitHub registries, npx shadcn eject to extract any component into your codebase
    • 📦 Next Intl 4.13 - useExtracted function now generates message keys using URL-safe base64
    • 📦 React Flow 12.11 - new autoPanOnSelection prop
    • 📦 XState Store 4.0 - createStoreLogic for reusable stores, enq.trigger for chaining events, Standard Schema support, store.can for event checking.
    • 📦 React Archer 5.0 - Draw arrows between elements - Add React 19 support
    • 📦 ESLint Plugin React ”You Might Not Need An Effect” 1.0
    • 🎥 Jack Herrington - All 17 TanStack Projects In ONE App!
    • 🎙️ Señors at Scale - Redux at Scale with Mark Erikson

    Don't miss the next email!

      💸 Sponsor​

      Ship AI generated code safely with Meticulous.

      Claude writes your code. Claude reviews your code. Claude fixes the review comments. And somehow, you're the one getting paged at 2am when it breaks in prod.

      Fortunately, top AI-driven teams like Dropbox, Notion, LaunchDarkly, and Wiz rely on Meticulous to run 1000s of e2e UI tests autonomously, covering every user flow, edge case, role and permutation. Built by ex-Palantir engineers, Meticulous gives you near-exhaustive coverage in weeks, without any developer effort.

      It works like magic in the background:

      • Near-exhaustive coverage on every test run
      • No test creation
      • No maintenance (seriously)
      • Zero flakes (built on a deterministic browser)

      Check it out - and see why one engineering leader at Dropbox said that “once we started using Meticulous, we couldn’t imagine working without it.”

      📱 React-Native​

      Highlights from App.js Conf 2026

      Last week, the community gathered at the App.js Conf happening in Kraków. We're highlighting three announcements here, since many of the others shipped over the past month and were demoed again on stage. Make sure to catch the rest of the talks through the live stream recordings (Day 1, Day 2).

      React Native Gesture Handler 3.0

      Major release rebuilt for the New Architecture, dropping Old Architecture support, introducing a new hook-based API which is React Compiler friendly, a new Touchable component with built-in animations and deeper Reanimated integration.

      Swift Package Manager support for React Native

      Meta + Expo previewed npx react-native spm, which scaffolds SPM config to replace CocoaPods: generates a committable Xcode project, runs codegen, caches XCFrameworks globally. CocoaPods trunk goes read-only Dec 2. Both Cocoapods and SPM work in parallel during the transition.

      EAS Observe

      New Expo production performance monitoring service that tracks real-world startup times, render performance, and bundle load times across devices, with release comparison and per-session investigation. Currently in Private Preview, free up to 10K MAU for at least three months.

      • 💸 Maestro MCP - Let your AI agent write and run mobile UI tests for you
      • 🐦 N-API v10 support landed in Hermes trunk - native Node-API addons can now run on Hermes without modification. Still experimental.
      • 🗓️ Chain React - 🇺🇸 Portland - 30-31 July. The US React Native conference is back! Grab your tickets now and get a 15% discount with code "TWIR"!
      • 📜 Things I Learned While Building Expo - Evan Bacon is leaving Expo after 9 years. He wrote what he learned on dogfooding, listening user feedback, building developer trust through rough patches, and why data beats vibes when you're building developer tools.
      • 📜 Expo Router v56: Decoupling from React Navigation - Covers the decision to fork React Navigation internals for full architectural control, codemod-assisted migration, streaming SSR with generateMetadata, and new Android toolbar API and Native Tabs customization.
      • 📜 Native code in Expo SDK 56: inline modules and type generation - How inline modules let you write Swift and Kotlin files directly alongside your app code, and how expo-type-information automatically generates matching TypeScript interfaces from them.
      • 📦 React Native Runtimes - Run RN Components and business logic in isolated Hermes runtimes - This Margelo/Callstack library permits rendering React components on separate JS Runtimes, and share state across them.
      • 📦 Expo Desktop - build macOS and Windows desktop app with Expo - Unofficial announcement coming from the community.
      • 📦 React Native Doctor - New open-source CLI that scans your React Native app for performance and security bugs and fixes them with AI agents
      • 📦 TanStack AI 0.23 - React Native support - Chat clients and framework hooks now run in React Native via a new fetcher option.
      • 📦 Keyframer.dev - Visual Animation Editor for React Native - Outputs production-ready Reanimated v4 code - drag keyframes on a timeline, or type a prompt and get a useAnimatedScene hook you can paste directly into your project.
      • 📦 Legend List 3.0 - Web support added, scroll jump prevention is now core behavior, and maintainVisibleContentPosition is now opt-in and disabled by default.
      • 📦 Rozenite 1.11 - Network activity timeline & export, performance startup insights, waterfall view, navigation dispatch origin tracking
      • 📦 @swmansion/react-native-bottom-sheet - Native bottom-sheet components, inline and modal, with content-based sizing and scrollable-child handling
      • 📦 Sentry React Native 8.13 - NavigationContainer wrapper for React Navigation, iOS Swift Package Manager support, Expo Router param extraction, pause/resume app hang tracking
      • 📦 Argent 0.9 - new screenshot-diff too, configurable release-age-aware update reminders
      • 📦 Agent Device 0.16 - Maestro YAML replay compatibility, structured MCP command tools, iOS transform gestures, Android ANR recovery
      • 📦 Uniwind 1.8 - Vite 8 support, uniwind generate-artifacts CLI
      • 📦 React Native Purchases 10.2 - Ad tracking support
      • 🎥 Code with Beto - Why AI-Built Apps Feel Cheap (And How to Fix It)
      • 🎥 Expo - Where is Expo Go?

      🔀 Other​

      • 📊 State of CSS 2026 - Survey is now open
      • 📜 Node.js streams, backpressure, memory leaks - A pragmatic 2-part deep dive to help you reduce the memory footprint of your streams.
      • 📜 On Rendering Diffs – Pierre Computer Company - Deep dive into building a zero-blanking diff virtualizer using the "Inverse Sticky Technique", with insights on layout estimation, DOM pooling, memory optimization, and deferred syntax highlighting.
      • 📦 Node.js 26.3 - New permission.drop() API, configurable HTTP header validation, Buffer.poolSize bumped to 64 KiB - Improves the security model, letting you start with the permissions you need for initialization, then drop the ones you no longer need at runtime.
      • 📦 npm 11.16 - Phase 1 of package.json allowScripts - npm is rolling out supply chain security improvements in 2 phases, catching up with other package managers. v11.16 adds package.allowScripts=[] and warns on unapproved postinstall scripts. v12.0 will block them by default.
      • 📦 Aube 1.17 - Rust-based package manager - Staged publish trust, hoisting limits, remote tarball integrity fixes, and OTP support for dist-tag writes
      • 📦 pnpm 11.5 - hoistingLimits setting, interactive prompt library, detect staged publishing trust downgrades
      • 📦 Astro 6.4 - Pluggable markdown processor, Rust-based Markdown processor for faster builds, and Cloudflare helpers for advanced routing.
      • 📦 Yarn 4.16 - New stage command for staged publishing, editor SDK support for oxc

      🤭 Fun​

      See ya! 👋
